item.title: Yiggish!
item.content:encoded:
I feel a vague resemblance with Lovecraft's The Curse of Yig.
Well done, anyway, on the line between horrorific and plain weird.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Did she, though?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, it's competently done, but I was hoping for more than "she has a snake in her stomach." Towards the end, I was neutral-leaning-towards-downvote, but the "she angered an Aztec god" was such a bad twist that it soured me on the whole thing. Down vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hello! Shouldn't the object class be Euclid instead of Safe, since she is a humaniod scip? Overall a very creepy scip!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nope, it's replaced by an entire, living snake. :B

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one - have an upvote.
Just a question though, when you say that her tongue, stomach, and esophagus are replaced by the snake, do you mean that it’s like a living, whole snake, rather than just those respective organs(i.e she’s got a forked tongue, like D&D’s Yuan-ti)?
It’s a little unclear in the beginning, but that may just be me misunderstanding what was written.

I probably wouldn’t worry about changing that, since I probably just misunderstood it. Rereading it clears things up.

item.title: Re: Happy +100
item.content:encoded: It's back down to 99. Again. :( But I thank you, nevertheless!

item.title: Happy +100
item.content:encoded:
It may have taken a while, but I am happy to provide the hundredth. :D

What gets me is the symbiotic nature of this SCP, the snake truly is a part of her, and it (sort of) helps her by making her digestion more efficient. The only thing I would note, having raised many herps, is that you would be using thawed flash frozen rodents. Freeze drying an animal is basically mumification.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You don't thaw something freeze-dried, you rehydrate it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, weird, concise and well written. +1, well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The last time I edited this, I somehow left out a very important word.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Shouldn't the anomalous item that made her a monster be the SCP?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seriously? What was the point of it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was totally a D&D spell

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Chick-who-bleeds-snakes writer here. I wouldn't be concerned about the two articles having the same inspiration. The only thing linking 486 to any myth is its name, and I've always figured that those are unofficial nicknames in-universe.

The nature of their mutations are plenty distinct (1132 is much more extreme), so no worries. At the very least, it's affecting them at different ends. As it were.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I shivered when reading this. Twice. Take all my upvotes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And now I have also upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it too explainy still?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It seems to have taken an upturn. Still not as good as my last showing, but I can live with this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yay you fixed that last bit. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sort of, but at the same time the article is doing very well and it seems people like it. It might not be the best feeling for an author to use an existing premise but if it works then it works, right? If I was you I'd be very happy it's going so well rather than dwelling on what's been done before.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, er, uh…

Well, you know what I mean, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, she's kind of indigenous Mexican. And I was thinking more Hannibal Lecter, though I have no idea what actually exists and would be usable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, if you want an image of a Japanese young woman wearing a cloth air-filter mask, search for Kuchisake-onna ("slit-mouthed woman").

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Saving? It's at +16.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm really concerned about this now. I didn't know that one existed, and if I had, I wouldn't have gone forward with this. Maybe this one's not worth saving. I'm torn.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Quite a while. In fact, it's still there because I'm lazy about removing things. Maybe I'll go do that now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: *cough*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This kind of reminds me of the woman who bleeds snakes, but I like it.

EDIT: And of course I'm not the only one. xD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Exactly. The researchers are trying to determine the chain of events. She broke an icon, and became cursed. I chewed some gum, and my blood foamed out my pores-we wouldn't believe that the God of Double-Bubble cursed me, even if I think so. It would just be a name applied. It doesn't mean that it truly exists.

I remember the original Star Trek episode with Adonis. Just because he thought he was a god, and ancient humans thought he was a god, doesn't mean he was. He just had, to paraphrase Clarke, a technology indistinguishable from magic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Do I have severe déja vu or was this floating in a sandbox for a while?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if the whole "breaking a sacred object" thing is only what she believes is the cause of her snakyness?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wearing the mask is not a choice, if she wants to be fed, and she does need to eat, which is why it can't be on all the time. But you can't approach her and be assured safety unless you're, I don't know, wearing a suit of magical snake-proof armor, so she has to keep herself contained in that sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one doesn't seem to be doing very well.

The only real feedback I got on this via sandbox was "It needs an interview". Originally, there was an addendum spelling out what's in the interview, but impartially and from the perspective of having talked with people in this woman's tribe. It seems like more people don't like the interview than like it, but I'm not sure if taking it out wholesale would improve things, if going back to the addendum would, or what. I honestly don't know what to do.

But that's what I get for not getting feedback on the interview until posting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh, I wonder how that happened. I also have no colons after the Assistant Researcher's name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dammit, shit. This makes me very sad. Can we just say that Coatlicue is a very unforgiving goddess?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The interview could be a little less overt, or the article could build on the interview some more.

However, I don't like the concept. Coupled with the suddenness of the interview, and -1. Sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now I want to see the Containment Procedures for Kali-Yuga, or even Anansi…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this one up until the "She was CURSED BY THE GODS!" part at the end. In my opinion, religious-themed SCPs are well and good (after all, i've written a Catholic fox and a 500-year-old Buddhist mummy who can't die), but one should really stop short of implying that any of the gods people are worshipping are real, and I might be mistaken but i'm pretty sure the only people worshipping Aztec gods in this day and age are neopagans. The Foundation's cosmology is crowded enough without having to put Thor, Kali-Yuga, and Horus-Re under containment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I also like this idea. It makes more sense than "Oh yeah, a god did it, duh."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Assistant is a little bit glib about it, but I definitely agree here. She's stating her belief about why this has happened to her, not the scientifically proven explanation. All we concretely know is that some time before the snake thing happened, she broke an idol of this serpent god. Maybe the idol was the real anomalous item. Maybe the people of her tribe got pissed, snuck in one night, and replaced her digestive tract with a snake. We don't actually know.

I'd fix the line about her uterus being removed, though, that's almost a classic example of bad clinical tone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this and don't mind the interview either, but it would not be a bad idea to build on the issues the interview brings up, even if it is a lolfoundationy note from some head researcher that says the goddess-did-it explanation is bupkiss.

Hmm, or better yet an implication that she was exposed to "SCP-[blackedout]" which could just mean that the subtext of the interview is actually "OH! Is that what did this to her? We've got it locked up down the hall, no big deal" instead of "Oh, a god did it!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dammit, that's pretty much exactly what I was gonna say :(

In all honesty, having it explained that she's that way because an ancient Mesoamerican god got pissed that she broke its idol would probably only lead to more testing and experimentation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not if you know where to go on the internet, but that's a part of the internet I stay away from. What with the viruses, hacks, exploits…. Not to mention all the stuff it can do to your computer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know, I kind of like the interview. Yes it spells it out, but who's to say that's the actual truth behind the matter? What if the relic itself was responsible? In fact with if the anomalous relic was what caused the god to be worshiped in the first place? You can't say "Oh a junior research assistant says a god did it, case closed."

Although if that is the case, [REDACTED] it at least we have one SCP with the mysticism behind it partially explained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interview: Are you talking to me? No, I don't. She says a god did this to her?

Yeah, I don't think the Interview should be able to talk, it's a concept and not people.

I also really dislike the interview for pretty much blatantly punching the reader in the face with the entire backstory. You could've ended the interview with both researchers drawing a blank on the appropriate snake-themed god and the interview would still do it's job of explaining the backstory with no subtlety or finesse in the slightest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interview sinks it. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't like a lot of things about this one. Not saying it's bad; I just don't like it.

In the Containment Procedures, Jesus, I don't like it when the Foundation is portrayed as monster-day-care. Lock it up, feed it so it doesn't die, and take it's venom for testing. That's all the Foundation is obligated to do. Why even give her the choice to wear the damn mask? I'd rather she be locked in the damn thing so she doesn't forget and bite my head off during an 'interview'. Why do we need an interview anyway?

Well, there's your culprit. She broke an icon or something belonging to the serpent mother goddess Coatlicue, and now she's like this.

No. Just no. Not only is this unprofessional for a scientific research nerd, it's unrealistic as well. If you want to imply a religious idea, brand the god's face into her ass or something. This just feels too much like an Arachne imitation for me to suspend my disbelief.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One request - which religion should provide the icon? You wouldn't want to supply a snake-mouthed devout Catholic with a Star of David or something…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kind of like the interview. Sure, it removes some of the mystery, but it shows the Foundation actually succeeding in getting more information about something. I wouldn't want more interviews like that, but on at least one article, it's good to have. Also sets this apart from Coatlicue Skin more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you adjusted the interview at the end, I would upvote this. That's really the only thing I see wrong here. Downvoting for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The interview lays things out too overtly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-486. It's even called "Coatlicue Skin".

That said, I think this is sufficiently different and interesting that I'll upvote. I do agree that the interview is fairly… abrupt in its revelation of the cause.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just how the uterus was removed has not yet been determined.

This sentence doesn't quite "fit" with the clinical tone of the rest of the entry. Starting the sentence with "Just how" is the likely culprit for the break in style. I also agree that the interview is "a bit overt" and could be refined. Otherwise, I found this interesting and am inclined to start googling "women in masks" and other variants to see what can be found.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I lol'd. I honestly did. I'm so making a religious .ico now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one, it's sufficiently freaky without paying too much attention to itself. It's a shame the pictures of Shawnee Smith from the first Saw film are so well known else they would be perfect for this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1132 is to be kept in standard living quarters, furnished with a bed and religious icon.

one 64x64 pixel flying spaghetti monster, coming right up!

the concept is awesome. the interview is a bit overt; it would be more effective if there are indications that what the SCP believes happened isn't quite how it went down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And your little dog.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See, I like things a lot better when they're not spelled out for me. I like the fuckeduppedness involved in this, but it's not really enough on the whole to make me upvote. Not downvoting either though.
